rationale_for_baseline_tier: Acute massive vaginal haemorrhage with decompensated hypovolaemic shock (hypotension, marked tachycardia, diaphoresis, prolonged capillary refill) in a patient who is soaking a pad every ~20 minutes, feels faint and is alone. This is an immediate threat to life requiring emergency ambulance transfer for resuscitation, transfusion and haemostatic control — T5 (call 000). The patient can herself report the time-critical features (heavy bleeding, faintness, clamminess), so escalation does not depend on any clinician measurement. The underlying cervical malignancy cannot be confirmed remotely and is not what sets the tier — the haemorrhagic shock does.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Feeling faint/lightheaded, cold and clammy, or worsening dizziness on any movement
escalation_edges.rationale: Patient-reportable signs of shock indicate decompensated circulatory compromise from blood loss — an emergency ambulance is required immediately.
escalation_edges.communication_template: You're describing signs of serious blood loss. I'm arranging an emergency ambulance (000) now. Lie flat, raise your legs on pillows, and stay still and warm.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Continued heavy vaginal bleeding soaking pads, large clots, or severe pelvic/flank pain
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — do not wait to see if it settles
escalation_edges.rationale: Ongoing rapid blood loss risks exsanguination and requires hospital haemostasis and transfusion; the flank pain also signals possible obstructive renal injury needing urgent imaging.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because the bleeding is this heavy and not stopping, you need to go to hospital by ambulance now — don't wait to see if it eases off.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Collapse or loss of consciousness while alone at home
escalation_edges.rationale: Unwitnessed collapse in a bleeding, isolated patient is high-risk; the ambulance must be able to reach her.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Unlock your front door now so the paramedics can get in, and if you can, call a neighbour or family member to come. Stay on the line with me and 000.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no one present during an active haemorrhagic emergency
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Actively ensure the ambulance can access her: unlock the front door, keep her on the line, and if possible alert a neighbour or relative. Do not rely on her being able to open the door if she collapses.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Faint, actively bleeding and at high syncope risk
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: She must NOT drive or self-transport; an ambulance is mandatory because of the collapse risk en route.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an emergency ambulance right now for very heavy vaginal bleeding with signs of serious blood loss. While you wait: lie flat and raise your legs on pillows, keep warm, and stay as still as you can. Do not stand, walk or drive. Do not put anything inside the vagina — use pads only. Unlock the front door for the paramedics and stay on the phone.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge summary; the months of offensive discharge, postcoital/intermenstrual bleeding and unintentional weight loss must be handed over for urgent gynaecology-oncology assessment and biopsy.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating massive vaginal bleeding with faintness as a heavy period or minor gynae bleed and offering self-care or a routine/same-day GP appointment.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Unrecognised haemorrhagic shock progresses to collapse, organ hypoperfusion and death while the patient waits at home alone.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising it as urgent but advising self-transport to ED rather than a 000 ambulance; risks the patient fainting or exsanguinating en route without paramedic resuscitation.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Over-triage is not a meaningful risk here — T5 is correct; the failure mode of concern is under-action, not over-escalation.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The cause of the bleeding (suspected locally advanced cervical malignancy) cannot be confirmed remotely without examination, imaging and biopsy.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Act on the emergency regardless of cause — escalate for the shock, and refer the sinister background for urgent specialist work-up — without naming or excluding cancer.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Delaying escalation to establish the diagnosis, or conversely announcing a confirmed cancer diagnosis, over the phone.
